questions.question: What is C#?
questions.answer: C# is an object oriented programming language developed by Microsoft mainly used for building web applications, APIs, desktop applications, and backend services.
questions.simpleExample: C# is the main language we use to write .NET applications.

questions.question: What is CLR in .NET?
questions.answer: CLR stands for Common Language Runtime. It executes .NET code and handles memory management, exception handling, and garbage collection.
questions.simpleExample: CLR is like the engine that runs .NET code.

questions.question: What is the difference between value type and reference type?
questions.answer: Value types store actual data directly while reference types store memory address pointing to actual object.
questions.simpleExample: int stores value directly, object stores reference.

questions.question: Difference between var and dynamic?
questions.answer: var type is decided at compile time while dynamic type is decided at runtime.
questions.simpleExample: var is fixed after compile, dynamic can change while running.

questions.question: What is boxing and unboxing?
questions.answer: Boxing converts value type to object type and unboxing converts object back to value type.
questions.codeExample: int a = 10; object obj = a; int b = (int)obj;
questions.simpleExample: Wrapping int inside object is boxing.

questions.question: What is method overloading?
questions.answer: Method overloading means creating multiple methods with same name but different parameters.
questions.codeExample:
Add(int a,int b)
Add(int a,int b,int c)
questions.simpleExample: Same method name but different input.

questions.question: What is method overriding?
questions.answer: Method overriding means changing parent class method behavior in child class using virtual and override keywords.
questions.simpleExample: Child class changes parent method behavior.

questions.question: What is encapsulation?
questions.answer: Encapsulation means hiding internal data and exposing only required functionality using properties and methods.
questions.codeExample: private int age; public int Age { get; set; }
questions.simpleExample: Hide data and allow controlled access.

questions.question: What is inheritance?
questions.answer: Inheritance allows child class to reuse properties and methods from parent class.
questions.simpleExample: Child class gets parent functionality.

questions.question: What is polymorphism?
questions.answer: Polymorphism allows same method to behave differently depending on object or parameters.
questions.simpleExample: Same method, different behavior.

questions.question: What is abstraction?
questions.answer: Abstraction means hiding implementation details and showing only important functionality.
questions.simpleExample: User sees what action does, not internal logic.

questions.question: Difference between abstract class and interface?
questions.answer: Abstract class can have implementation and constructor while interface only defines contract.
questions.simpleExample: Interface defines what to do, abstract can partially implement.

questions.question: Can a class implement multiple interfaces?
questions.answer: Yes a class can implement multiple interfaces in C#.
questions.simpleExample: One class can follow multiple contracts.

questions.question: Difference between Array and List?
questions.answer: Array has fixed size while List is dynamic and can grow or shrink.
questions.codeExample: int[] arr = new int[5]; List<int> list = new();
questions.simpleExample: Array fixed, List flexible.

questions.question: What is Dictionary in C#?
questions.answer: Dictionary stores data as key value pairs and allows fast lookup using key.
questions.simpleExample: Store like ID and Name together.

questions.question: Difference between IEnumerable and IQueryable?
questions.answer: IEnumerable works in memory while IQueryable executes query on database side.
questions.simpleExample: IQueryable filters in DB, IEnumerable filters after data comes.

questions.question: What is Queue?
questions.answer: Queue follows FIFO structure meaning first in first out.
questions.simpleExample: First inserted item comes out first.

questions.question: What is Stack?
questions.answer: Stack follows LIFO structure meaning last in first out.
questions.simpleExample: Last inserted item comes out first.

questions.question: What is exception handling?
questions.answer: Exception handling allows application to handle runtime errors without crashing.
questions.simpleExample: Handle errors safely.

questions.question: What is try catch finally?
questions.answer: Try contains risky code, catch handles error, finally always executes.
questions.simpleExample: Finally runs whether error happens or not.

questions.question: Difference between throw and throw ex?
questions.answer: throw keeps original stack trace while throw ex resets stack trace.
questions.simpleExample: Use throw to preserve original error details.

questions.question: Why use finally block?
questions.answer: Finally is used for cleanup like closing DB connection or releasing resources.
questions.simpleExample: Always runs at end.

questions.question: How to create custom exception?
questions.answer: Create class inheriting Exception class.
questions.simpleExample: Create your own custom error.

questions.question: What is delegate in C#?
questions.answer: Delegate is a type safe function pointer that holds reference to methods.
questions.simpleExample: Delegate points to a method.

questions.question: What is event in C#?
questions.answer: Event allows one class to notify other classes when something happens.
questions.simpleExample: Button click triggers event.

questions.question: Difference between delegate and event?
questions.answer: Delegate can directly call methods while event is used for notifications and restricts direct invocation outside class.
questions.simpleExample: Event is safer wrapper around delegate.

questions.question: What is Action delegate?
questions.answer: Action is built in delegate that returns no value.
questions.simpleExample: Method runs but returns nothing.

questions.question: What is Func delegate?
questions.answer: Func is built in delegate that returns a value.
questions.simpleExample: Method takes input and returns result.

questions.question: What is ASP.NET Core?
questions.answer: ASP.NET Core is a cross platform framework used to build web applications, REST APIs, and backend services.
questions.simpleExample: Framework used for building backend APIs in .NET.

questions.question: Difference between .NET Framework and ASP.NET Core?
questions.answer: .NET Framework mainly runs on Windows while ASP.NET Core is cross platform and works on Windows, Linux, and Mac.
questions.simpleExample: .NET Core works everywhere while old framework mostly Windows.

questions.question: What is Kestrel server?
questions.answer: Kestrel is the built in web server in ASP.NET Core used to handle HTTP requests.
questions.simpleExample: Kestrel receives incoming API requests.

questions.question: What is request pipeline in ASP.NET Core?
questions.answer: Request pipeline is a sequence of middleware through which every request passes before reaching controller.
questions.simpleExample: Request passes multiple steps before API executes.

questions.question: Why is ASP.NET Core faster than older .NET Framework?
questions.answer: It has optimized runtime, lightweight pipeline, built in dependency injection, and better performance improvements.
questions.simpleExample: New architecture makes requests faster.

questions.question: What is Program.cs in ASP.NET Core?
questions.answer: Program.cs is the main entry point where services are registered and middleware pipeline is configured.
questions.simpleExample: Application starts execution from Program.cs.

questions.question: What does builder.Services do?
questions.answer: builder.Services is used to register services for dependency injection container.
questions.simpleExample: Register services so framework can inject them later.

questions.question: What does builder.Build() do?
questions.answer: builder.Build creates the application instance after service registration is complete.
questions.simpleExample: Creates actual application object.

questions.question: What does app.Run() do?
questions.answer: app.Run starts the application and begins listening for incoming requests.
questions.simpleExample: Starts web server and waits for requests.

questions.question: Difference between builder.Services and app?
questions.answer: builder.Services registers dependencies while app configures request pipeline and middleware.
questions.simpleExample: Services setup dependencies, app handles request flow.

questions.question: Why do we call AddControllers()?
questions.answer: AddControllers registers controller support so API endpoints can be created.
questions.simpleExample: Without it controller APIs will not work.

questions.question: What is middleware?
questions.answer: Middleware is a component in ASP.NET Core pipeline that processes request and response before reaching controller.
questions.simpleExample: Every request passes through middleware first.

questions.question: Why is middleware order important?
questions.answer: Middleware executes in sequence and wrong order can break authentication or routing.
questions.simpleExample: Wrong order means request may fail.

questions.question: Difference between Use, Run, and Map?
questions.answer: Use passes request to next middleware, Run ends pipeline, Map branches pipeline based on route.
questions.simpleExample: Use continues, Run stops, Map creates route branch.

questions.question: What does UseRouting() do?
questions.answer: UseRouting identifies which endpoint or controller should handle request.
questions.simpleExample: Routing decides which API endpoint will run.

questions.question: Can middleware stop request pipeline?
questions.answer: Yes if middleware does not call next delegate then request stops there.
questions.simpleExample: Middleware can block request from reaching controller.

questions.question: How do you create custom middleware?
questions.answer: Create a class with RequestDelegate and Invoke method then register it in pipeline.
questions.simpleExample: Custom middleware adds your own request logic.

questions.question: What is dependency injection?
questions.answer: Dependency injection is a design pattern where framework automatically provides required services instead of manually creating objects.
questions.simpleExample: Framework gives service automatically.

questions.question: Why use dependency injection?
questions.answer: It reduces tight coupling, improves testability, and makes code reusable.
questions.simpleExample: Controller does not create service manually.

questions.question: What is a dependency?
questions.answer: Dependency is any object or service another class needs to work properly.
questions.simpleExample: Controller depends on service.

questions.question: What is constructor injection?
questions.answer: Constructor injection means passing dependencies through constructor automatically by framework.
questions.simpleExample: Dependency comes when object is created.

questions.question: What is inversion of control?
questions.answer: Inversion of control means object creation responsibility moves from class to framework.
questions.simpleExample: Framework controls object creation instead of developer.

questions.question: What is Transient lifetime?
questions.answer: Transient creates a new instance every time service is requested.
questions.simpleExample: New object created every time.

questions.question: What is Scoped lifetime?
questions.answer: Scoped creates one instance per request and same instance is reused during request lifecycle.
questions.simpleExample: One object per API request.

questions.question: What is Singleton lifetime?
questions.answer: Singleton creates one instance for entire application and shares it everywhere.
questions.simpleExample: Same object reused for whole app.

questions.question: Why is DbContext usually scoped?
questions.answer: DbContext should be scoped because one database context should exist per request to avoid conflicts.
questions.simpleExample: Each request gets separate database context.

questions.question: What happens if Singleton stores user specific data?
questions.answer: Different users may accidentally share same data because singleton is shared globally.
questions.simpleExample: User data can leak between requests.

questions.question: What is a Controller in ASP.NET Core?
questions.answer: Controller is a class that handles incoming HTTP requests and returns responses to the client.
questions.simpleExample: Controller receives API request and sends response.

questions.question: What does ApiController attribute do?
questions.answer: ApiController enables automatic model validation, better API behavior, and automatic bad request responses.
questions.simpleExample: Helps framework treat class as API controller.

questions.question: What does Route attribute do?
questions.answer: Route attribute defines the URL path through which controller or action can be accessed.
questions.simpleExample: Defines API endpoint URL.

questions.question: What is ControllerBase?
questions.answer: ControllerBase is base class for API controllers and provides helper methods like Ok(), BadRequest(), NotFound().
questions.simpleExample: Base class used for APIs without views.

questions.question: What is IActionResult?
questions.answer: IActionResult allows controller to return different HTTP responses like success, error, or not found.
questions.simpleExample: Flexible response type for API methods.

questions.question: Difference between Ok() and BadRequest()?
questions.answer: Ok returns success response while BadRequest returns client error when request data is invalid.
questions.simpleExample: Ok means success, BadRequest means invalid input.

questions.question: What is routing in ASP.NET Core?
questions.answer: Routing maps incoming URL requests to correct controller and action methods.
questions.simpleExample: Routing decides which API method should execute.

questions.question: What is attribute routing?
questions.answer: Attribute routing defines routes directly using attributes above controller or methods.
questions.simpleExample: Routes are written directly on methods.

questions.question: What is conventional routing?
questions.answer: Conventional routing defines route patterns centrally instead of inside controller attributes.
questions.simpleExample: Routes managed from one place.

questions.question: Can multiple routes point to same controller?
questions.answer: Yes multiple route attributes can be applied to same controller or action.
questions.simpleExample: One API method can respond to multiple URLs.

questions.question: What is route parameter?
questions.answer: Route parameter allows dynamic values in URL like ID or category.
questions.simpleExample: Pass values directly inside URL.

questions.question: What is HTTP GET method?
questions.answer: GET method is used to retrieve data from server without modifying anything.
questions.simpleExample: Fetch data from API.

questions.question: What is POST method?
questions.answer: POST method sends new data to server and usually creates a new record.
questions.simpleExample: Send new data to server.

questions.question: What is PUT method?
questions.answer: PUT method updates complete existing resource by replacing all fields.
questions.simpleExample: Replace entire record.

questions.question: What is PATCH method?
questions.answer: PATCH method updates only specific fields instead of replacing entire object.
questions.simpleExample: Update only changed fields.

questions.question: What is DELETE method?
questions.answer: DELETE method removes a resource from server or database.
questions.simpleExample: Delete existing record.

questions.question: Difference between PUT and PATCH?
questions.answer: PUT replaces full object while PATCH updates only selected properties.
questions.codeExample: PUT full object, PATCH partial object
questions.simpleExample: PUT = replace everything, PATCH = update only changes.

questions.question: What is model binding?
questions.answer: Model binding automatically maps incoming request data to controller method parameters or models.
questions.simpleExample: Framework converts request data into object automatically.

questions.question: What does FromBody do?
questions.answer: FromBody tells framework to read data from request body and convert it into object.
questions.simpleExample: Read JSON data sent from frontend.

questions.question: What does FromQuery do?
questions.answer: FromQuery reads data from query string parameters in URL.
questions.simpleExample: Reads values after question mark in URL.

questions.question: What does FromRoute do?
questions.answer: FromRoute reads values passed directly inside URL route.
questions.simpleExample: Reads dynamic route values.

questions.question: Can model binding automatically validate model?
questions.answer: Yes with ApiController and validation attributes framework automatically validates model.
questions.simpleExample: Framework checks required fields automatically.

questions.question: What is HTTP status code 200?
questions.answer: Status code 200 means request completed successfully.
questions.simpleExample: Everything worked successfully.

questions.question: What is status code 201?
questions.answer: Status code 201 means new resource was created successfully.
questions.simpleExample: New record created successfully.

questions.question: What is status code 400?
questions.answer: Status code 400 means client sent invalid request data.
questions.simpleExample: Something wrong in request.

questions.question: What is status code 401?
questions.answer: Status code 401 means user is not authenticated and login is required.
questions.simpleExample: User needs valid token or login.

questions.question: Difference between 401 and 403?
questions.answer: 401 means user is not authenticated while 403 means user is authenticated but not allowed.
questions.simpleExample: 401 = no login, 403 = no permission.

questions.question: What is status code 500?
questions.answer: Status code 500 means internal server error happened on backend.
questions.simpleExample: Backend crashed due to error.

questions.question: What is Entity Framework Core?
questions.answer: Entity Framework Core is an ORM used in .NET to interact with database using C# classes instead of writing raw SQL queries.
questions.simpleExample: Write C# code instead of manually writing SQL queries.

questions.question: What is ORM?
questions.answer: ORM stands for Object Relational Mapping and maps database tables to C# classes.
questions.simpleExample: Database table becomes C# object.

questions.question: What is DbContext?
questions.answer: DbContext is the main class in EF Core used to connect and communicate with database.
questions.simpleExample: DbContext is bridge between application and database.

questions.question: What is DbSet?
questions.answer: DbSet represents a database table and allows querying and saving data.
questions.simpleExample: DbSet works like a table in database.

questions.question: What is migration in EF Core?
questions.answer: Migration is used to update database schema when model classes change.
questions.simpleExample: Migration updates database structure.

questions.question: What does SaveChanges do?
questions.answer: SaveChanges sends all tracked changes to database permanently.
questions.simpleExample: Actually saves insert, update, delete into database.

questions.question: Why is DbContext scoped?
questions.answer: DbContext should be scoped so each request gets separate database connection context.
questions.simpleExample: Each API request gets separate DB context.

questions.question: What is LINQ?
questions.answer: LINQ allows querying collections or databases using C# syntax instead of writing loops or SQL manually.
questions.simpleExample: Filter and query data using C#.

questions.question: What does Where method do in LINQ?
questions.answer: Where filters data based on a condition.
questions.simpleExample: Only returns matching records.

questions.question: What does Select do in LINQ?
questions.answer: Select projects data into a different shape or selects specific properties.
questions.simpleExample: Pick only required fields.

questions.question: Difference between FirstOrDefault and SingleOrDefault?
questions.answer: FirstOrDefault returns first matching record while SingleOrDefault expects only one matching record and throws error if multiple found.
questions.simpleExample: First gets first match, Single expects exactly one.

questions.question: Difference between IEnumerable and IQueryable?
questions.answer: IEnumerable works in memory while IQueryable builds query and executes directly on database.
questions.simpleExample: IQueryable filters before data comes from DB.

questions.question: What does OrderBy do?
questions.answer: OrderBy sorts data in ascending order.
questions.simpleExample: Sort records alphabetically.

questions.question: Difference between Any and All?
questions.answer: Any checks if at least one record matches while All checks if every record matches.
questions.simpleExample: Any = one match, All = every match.

questions.question: What is Repository Pattern?
questions.answer: Repository Pattern separates database logic from business logic by creating separate repository classes.
questions.simpleExample: Keep database code separate from controller.

questions.question: Why use Repository Pattern?
questions.answer: It improves code organization, testability, and keeps controllers clean.
questions.simpleExample: Database logic stays in one place.

questions.question: What layer usually calls repository?
questions.answer: Service layer usually calls repository and controller calls service.
questions.simpleExample: Controller should not directly handle database logic.

questions.question: Can repository directly use DbContext?
questions.answer: Yes repository usually injects DbContext to perform database operations.
questions.simpleExample: Repository talks directly to database.

questions.question: What is advantage of using interface with repository?
questions.answer: Interface improves testability and allows easy replacement of implementation.
questions.simpleExample: Can swap implementation easily.

questions.question: What is async in C#?
questions.answer: Async keyword allows method to run asynchronously without blocking main execution thread.
questions.simpleExample: Method runs without freezing application.

questions.question: What is await keyword?
questions.answer: Await waits for asynchronous operation to complete without blocking thread.
questions.simpleExample: Wait for task to finish while thread remains free.

questions.question: Why use async await?
questions.answer: It improves performance by freeing thread while waiting for slow operations like database or API calls.
questions.simpleExample: Application handles more requests efficiently.

questions.question: Can await be used without async?
questions.answer: No await can only be used inside async method.
questions.simpleExample: Await needs async method.

questions.question: Why use ToListAsync instead of ToList?
questions.answer: ToListAsync performs database operation asynchronously and avoids blocking request thread.
questions.simpleExample: Better for API performance.

questions.question: What is Task in C#?
questions.answer: Task represents asynchronous operation and is commonly used with async await.
questions.simpleExample: Task represents work running asynchronously.

questions.question: What is Thread in C#?
questions.answer: Thread is an actual execution path managed by operating system.
questions.simpleExample: Thread does actual execution work.

questions.question: Difference between Task and Thread?
questions.answer: Thread is low level execution unit while Task is higher level abstraction for asynchronous operations.
questions.simpleExample: Task is lightweight wrapper around asynchronous work.

questions.question: Why prefer Task over Thread?
questions.answer: Task uses thread pool internally and is more efficient than manually creating threads.
questions.simpleExample: Task manages resources better.

questions.question: Does async await create new thread?
questions.answer: Not always, async await mainly frees current thread while waiting and may reuse thread pool.
questions.simpleExample: Async does not automatically mean new thread.

questions.question: What is authentication?
questions.answer: Authentication is the process of verifying who the user is before allowing access to the application.
questions.simpleExample: System checks whether user is valid or not.

questions.question: Why do we need authentication in APIs?
questions.answer: Authentication ensures only valid users can access protected APIs and prevents unauthorized access.
questions.simpleExample: Only logged in users can use API.

questions.question: What are common authentication methods?
questions.answer: Common methods are JWT token, cookies, OAuth, API keys, and session based authentication.
questions.simpleExample: Different ways to verify user identity.

questions.question: What is Bearer token?
questions.answer: Bearer token is an access token sent in HTTP Authorization header to prove user identity.
questions.simpleExample: Frontend sends token with every request.

questions.question: What middleware handles authentication?
questions.answer: UseAuthentication middleware validates incoming token or authentication method before request reaches controller.
questions.simpleExample: Middleware checks if user is valid.

questions.question: What is JWT?
questions.answer: JWT stands for JSON Web Token and is used to securely transfer user identity information between client and server.
questions.simpleExample: Token proves user is already logged in.

questions.question: What are the three parts of JWT?
questions.answer: JWT has Header, Payload, and Signature parts separated by dots.
questions.simpleExample: JWT contains info plus security signature.

questions.question: What is payload in JWT?
questions.answer: Payload stores user related information like user ID, role, or email.
questions.simpleExample: Stores user details inside token.

questions.question: What is JWT signature?
questions.answer: Signature ensures token was not modified and verifies authenticity using secret key.
questions.simpleExample: Protects token from tampering.

questions.question: Where is JWT usually stored?
questions.answer: JWT is commonly stored in localStorage, sessionStorage, or secure HTTP cookies.
questions.simpleExample: Frontend stores token after login.

questions.question: What happens when JWT expires?
questions.answer: When token expires server rejects request and user usually needs refresh token or re-login.
questions.simpleExample: Expired token means login again.

questions.question: What is authorization?
questions.answer: Authorization decides what authenticated user is allowed to access inside application.
questions.simpleExample: Checks what user can do after login.

questions.question: Difference between authentication and authorization?
questions.answer: Authentication checks who user is while authorization checks what user is allowed to access.
questions.simpleExample: Login first, permission check second.

questions.question: What does Authorize attribute do?
questions.answer: Authorize attribute protects controller or action so only authenticated users can access it.
questions.simpleExample: Blocks access for unauthenticated users.

questions.question: What is role based authorization?
questions.answer: Role based authorization allows access only to users having specific roles like Admin or User.
questions.simpleExample: Only admins can access this API.

questions.question: Why must UseAuthentication come before UseAuthorization?
questions.answer: Authentication must run first because authorization needs authenticated user information to check permissions.
questions.simpleExample: Need login check before permission check.

questions.question: Why is logging important?
questions.answer: Logging helps track application behavior, debug errors, and monitor issues in production.
questions.simpleExample: Helps understand what happened inside app.

questions.question: What is ILogger in ASP.NET Core?
questions.answer: ILogger is built in logging service used to log information, warnings, and errors.
questions.simpleExample: Built in service for writing logs.

questions.question: Different types of logs?
questions.answer: Common log types are Information, Warning, Error, Debug, and Critical.
questions.simpleExample: Different log levels based on severity.

questions.question: Where can logs be stored?
questions.answer: Logs can be stored in console, text files, database, cloud services, or monitoring systems.
questions.simpleExample: Logs can be stored in many places.

questions.question: Why log exceptions?
questions.answer: Logging exceptions helps identify bugs and understand what caused application failure.
questions.simpleExample: Know why backend crashed.

questions.question: What is global exception handling?
questions.answer: Global exception handling catches unhandled exceptions in one central place instead of writing try catch everywhere.
questions.simpleExample: Handle all backend errors from one place.

questions.question: Why avoid try catch in every controller?
questions.answer: Repeating try catch everywhere creates duplicate code and makes application harder to maintain.
questions.simpleExample: One central error handler is cleaner.

questions.question: How can middleware handle exceptions globally?
questions.answer: Custom middleware catches exceptions before response is sent and returns standard error response.
questions.simpleExample: Middleware catches all unexpected errors.

questions.question: What status code is returned for unhandled exception?
questions.answer: Unhandled exceptions usually return HTTP status code 500 internal server error.
questions.simpleExample: Backend error usually returns 500.

questions.question: Why return standard error response format?
questions.answer: Standard format helps frontend consistently handle errors and makes debugging easier.
questions.simpleExample: Frontend always gets predictable error structure.

questions.question: What is BackgroundService in ASP.NET Core?
questions.answer: BackgroundService allows code to run continuously in background without waiting for API requests.
questions.simpleExample: Runs tasks even when no API request comes.

questions.question: When do we use BackgroundService?
questions.answer: It is used for scheduled jobs, email sending, queue processing, and long running tasks.
questions.simpleExample: Useful for automatic tasks running in background.

questions.question: How do we register BackgroundService?
questions.answer: Register it using AddHostedService inside Program.cs.
questions.simpleExample: Register worker service during startup.

questions.question: Can BackgroundService access database?
questions.answer: Yes but scoped services should be created carefully using IServiceScopeFactory.
questions.simpleExample: Background worker can access DB safely with scope.

questions.question: Difference between API request and BackgroundService?
questions.answer: API runs when user sends request while BackgroundService runs independently in background.
questions.simpleExample: API waits for user, worker runs automatically.

questions.question: What is caching?
questions.answer: Caching stores frequently used data temporarily so application can return results faster without hitting database repeatedly.
questions.simpleExample: Store repeated data temporarily for faster response.

questions.question: Why use caching?
questions.answer: Caching improves performance and reduces unnecessary database calls.
questions.simpleExample: Avoid calling DB every time.

questions.question: What is MemoryCache?
questions.answer: MemoryCache stores temporary data directly inside application memory.
questions.simpleExample: Cache stored in server RAM.

questions.question: When should caching be avoided?
questions.answer: Caching should be avoided when data changes very frequently or must always remain real time.
questions.simpleExample: Do not cache constantly changing values.

questions.question: What is cache expiration?
questions.answer: Cache expiration automatically removes cached data after configured time limit.
questions.simpleExample: Cached data automatically disappears after time.

questions.question: What is Swagger?
questions.answer: Swagger is a tool used to document APIs and test endpoints directly from browser.
questions.simpleExample: Interactive API testing interface.

questions.question: Why use Swagger in API development?
questions.answer: Swagger helps developers understand endpoints, request models, and test APIs easily.
questions.simpleExample: Frontend team can test APIs easily.

questions.question: How do you enable Swagger?
questions.answer: Enable it by adding AddSwaggerGen service and UseSwagger middleware.
questions.simpleExample: Register service then enable middleware.

questions.question: Can Swagger test secured APIs?
questions.answer: Yes Swagger supports JWT authentication and secured endpoint testing.
questions.simpleExample: Swagger can send Bearer token.

questions.question: What is API versioning?
questions.answer: API versioning allows multiple versions of same API to exist without breaking old clients.
questions.simpleExample: Old frontend keeps working after API changes.

questions.question: Why do we need API versioning?
questions.answer: It allows backend changes without forcing all existing clients to update immediately.
questions.simpleExample: New changes do not break old apps.

questions.question: How can API version be passed?
questions.answer: Version can be passed through URL, headers, or query parameters.
questions.simpleExample: Version tells which API logic to use.

questions.question: What happens if API versioning is not used?
questions.answer: Frontend applications may break when backend changes response structure.
questions.simpleExample: Old apps stop working after backend updates.

questions.question: What is unit testing?
questions.answer: Unit testing checks individual methods or components independently to verify expected behavior.
questions.simpleExample: Test one method separately.

questions.question: Why do we write unit tests?
questions.answer: Unit tests help catch bugs early and ensure code changes do not break existing functionality.
questions.simpleExample: Verify code still works after changes.

questions.question: What is mocking in unit testing?
questions.answer: Mocking creates fake dependencies so method can be tested without real database or services.
questions.simpleExample: Create fake service for testing.

questions.question: Why mock dependencies?
questions.answer: Mocking isolates method logic and avoids calling real external systems during testing.
questions.simpleExample: Test logic without real DB.

questions.question: How do you optimize API performance?
questions.answer: Optimize by using async methods, caching, proper indexing, pagination, and reducing unnecessary database queries.
questions.simpleExample: Reduce slow operations and improve response time.

questions.question: Why use pagination in APIs?
questions.answer: Pagination avoids returning huge datasets and improves API speed and memory usage.
questions.simpleExample: Return small chunks instead of everything.

questions.question: Why avoid unnecessary database calls?
questions.answer: Repeated database calls increase latency and reduce backend performance.
questions.codeExample: Use cache instead of repeated queries
questions.simpleExample: Less DB calls means faster API.

questions.question: How can indexes improve performance?
questions.answer: Indexes allow database to find data faster instead of scanning entire table.
questions.simpleExample: Database searches become faster.

questions.question: Why use async for database calls?
questions.answer: Async frees request thread while waiting for database so server can handle more users.
questions.simpleExample: Improves scalability under high traffic.
